q: How does MoneyGram fit into Stellar's anchor model for cash-in/cash-out?

must_have.claim: MoneyGram Ramps is an anchor implementation providing fiat on/off ramps (cash-in/cash-out) on Stellar.
It runs on the Stellar Ramps standard so wallets get one integration to many cash points.

should_have.claim: Uses Stellar USDC as the on-chain settlement asset between the cash legs.
Wallets integrate via SEP-24 (hosted deposit/withdrawal) with SEP-10 auth; one integration reaches all MoneyGram locations.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes the wallet does not need to know the underlying bank/cash rails, and users need no bank account.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT claim MoneyGram runs its own Stellar validator or issues its own L1 token.
Do NOT fabricate a partnership detail or a settlement asset not backed by the corpus (it is USDC, not a MoneyGram coin).

q: What is the Stellar Anchor Platform and which SEPs does it implement?

must_have.claim: The Anchor Platform is SDF's (Java) backend/toolset for deploying a SEP-compliant anchor (on/off-ramp) service.
It implements the anchor SEP set: SEP-1, SEP-6, SEP-10, SEP-12, SEP-24, SEP-31, and SEP-38 (plus SEP-45).

should_have.claim: It is the fastest way to go live as an anchor without re-implementing the SEP endpoints.

nice_to_have.claim: Distinguishes it from the (TypeScript) Wallet SDK, which is the wallet-side counterpart.

q: What is an anchor on Stellar?

must_have.claim: An anchor is an on/off-ramp bridging the Stellar network to traditional financial rails (fiat).
Anchors accept fiat deposits and issue equivalent Stellar tokens, and redeem tokens back to real-world value.

should_have.claim: Anchors are typically financial institutions or fintech companies.
They implement standard SEPs so any compliant wallet can interoperate with them.

nice_to_have.claim: Gives an example anchor (e.g. MoneyGram Ramps).

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT define an anchor as a validator, a smart contract, or a consensus node.

q: List the account-level authorization flags an asset issuer can set on Stellar and what each one does.

must_have.claim: Names AUTH_REQUIRED (holders must be explicitly authorized before holding/receiving the asset).
Names AUTH_REVOCABLE (issuer can revoke/deauthorize a trustline).
Names AUTH_CLAWBACK_ENABLED (issuer can claw back / burn balances).
Names AUTH_IMMUTABLE (locks the flags so they cannot be weakened).

should_have.claim: Notes AUTH_CLAWBACK_ENABLED requires AUTH_REVOCABLE to also be set.

nice_to_have.claim: Mentions flags are set via SetOptions / SetTrustLineFlags operations.

Do NOT omit AUTH_CLAWBACK_ENABLED or conflate clawback with revocation as the same flag.

q: What is a claimable balance on Stellar and when would you use one instead of a direct payment?

must_have.claim: A claimable balance is a ledger entry that holds an amount until a designated claimant claims it (conditional/deferred transfer).
Useful when the recipient has no trustline yet or for escrow/predicate-gated payouts.

should_have.claim: Created via CreateClaimableBalance and claimed via ClaimClaimableBalance, with optional claim predicates (e.g. time bounds).

nice_to_have.claim: Notes a claimable balance entry holds a base reserve until claimed.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT describe a claimable balance as a smart contract escrow that requires Soroban.
Do NOT claim it bypasses the recipient's need to eventually establish a trustline before claiming a non-native asset.

q: If an asset issuer enables clawback, what does that mean for the asset's censorship-resistance, and why does Stellar support it anyway?

must_have.claim: An asset whose issuer can clawback is, by design, not censorship-resistant — the issuer can burn holders' balances.
Stellar supports clawback to make the network viable for regulated assets/securities where actual asset removal (not just freezing) is required.

should_have.claim: This is a deliberate trade-off: regulatory compliance vs. decentralization/trust-minimization.
Clawback is opt-in (requires AUTH_CLAWBACK_ENABLED + AUTH_REVOCABLE); a holder can inspect the flags before trusting the asset.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes XLM (native) is not clawback-able; this applies to issued assets.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT claim clawback makes the asset more decentralized or trustless.
Do NOT claim every Stellar asset is clawback-able regardless of issuer flags.

must_cite: The clawback page on developers.stellar.org or CAP-0035.
notes: Design-tension comparison. Dossier §8.2(2). Verified: clawback = CAP-0035 'Asset Clawback' (Final), activated at protocol 17; requires AUTH_CLAWBACK_ENABLED_FLAG (which itself requires AUTH_REVOCABLE_FLAG).
criteria: Raven SHOULD answer. Expected cards: stellar_docs_mcp (or any acceptable alternative — route-pass-equivalent: scout_research). MUST include (gate): An asset whose issuer can clawback is, by design, not censorship-resistant — the issuer can burn holders' balances.; Stellar supports clawback to make the network viable for regulated assets/securities where actual asset removal (not just freezing) is required.. MUST AVOID (gate): Do NOT claim clawback makes the asset more decentralized or trustless.; Do NOT claim every Stellar asset is clawback-able regardless of issuer flags.. Should include: This is a deliberate trade-off: regulatory compliance vs. decentralization/trust-minimization.; Clawback is opt-in (requires AUTH_CLAWBACK_ENABLED + AUTH_REVOCABLE); a holder can inspect the flags before trusting the asset.. Must cite: The clawback page on developers.stellar.org or CAP-0035..

q: How do I deploy the Stellar Asset Contract for an existing classic asset so I can use it from Soroban?

must_have.claim: You deploy the asset's SAC via the Stellar CLI (e.g. stellar contract asset deploy) or SDK, not by writing a new token contract.
The SAC contract ID is deterministically derived from the asset (CONTRACT_ID_FROM_ASSET); it represents the same classic asset.

should_have.claim: After deployment the issuer is automatically the admin (controls mint/clawback/set_authorized).
Mechanically it is an InvokeHostFunction with CREATE_CONTRACT host function.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes XLM's SAC is also derivable the same way.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT instruct the user to write/deploy a brand-new ERC-20-style token contract instead of deploying the asset's SAC.
Do NOT use a defunct command name (e.g. 'soroban token wrap' presented as the current canonical command without noting the current CLI).

q: Walk me through issuing a new custom token on Stellar from scratch.

must_have.claim: An asset is defined by its code + issuing account; there is no separate 'create asset' transaction — the asset exists once the issuer sends it.
The distribution account must establish a trustline (ChangeTrust) to the issuer, then the issuer sends (payment) the supply to it.

should_have.claim: Recommends the two-account (issuing + distribution) pattern and setting any needed auth flags first.
Recommends publishing a stellar.toml (SEP-1) so wallets can discover/identify the asset.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes optionally locking the issuer (AUTH_IMMUTABLE / setting master weight to 0) to cap supply.

Do NOT claim there is a dedicated 'createAsset' operation on the protocol.

q: How do path payments work on Stellar and which two operations implement them?

must_have.claim: Names PathPaymentStrictSend and PathPaymentStrictReceive as the two path-payment operations.
Explains a path payment converts one asset to another by routing across the DEX orderbook and/or AMM pools in a single atomic transaction.

should_have.claim: Strict-send fixes the source amount (receiver gets at least dest_min); strict-receive fixes the destination amount (sender pays at most send_max).
Settles in a few seconds (≈ one ledger close, ~5s) at low fee.

q: Compare the Stellar DEX (SDEX) orderbook with Stellar's AMM liquidity pools — how do they price trades and what are the cost differences?

must_have.claim: SDEX is an on-chain central-limit orderbook (offers via ManageBuy/SellOffer); AMM pools price via a constant-product (x*y=k) curve.
AMM swaps charge a fixed 30 basis points (0.30%) fee, separate from the network fee; orderbook offers can be priced tighter or wider.

should_have.claim: Both live in-ledger (no off-chain matching engine); path payments can route across both.
AMM pool-share trustlines cost two base reserves; LPs earn the accumulated fees proportional to their share.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes high-volume pairs tend to live on offers, exotic pairs on pools.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT state the AMM fee is a different number (e.g. 0.03%, 1%, 0.25%) as the canonical Stellar pool fee.

q: What is x402 on Stellar and how does it let APIs or AI agents charge per request?

must_have.claim: x402 is an open protocol from Coinbase Developer Platform that revives the HTTP 402 (Payment Required) status code for per-request payments.
On Stellar, x402 payments settle via Soroban authorization entries.
x402 uses SEP-41 tokens (USDC by default) as the payment asset.
A facilitator model (e.g. Coinbase's x402 facilitator, or the OpenZeppelin Relayer plugin) verifies and settles the signed payment via /verify, /settle, /supported endpoints.

should_have.claim: Enables APIs and AI agents to charge micro-payments per HTTP request without prior account setup.

Do NOT claim x402 requires API keys, pre-existing accounts, or a native gas token to pay per request.
Do NOT conflate x402 with MPP (Machine Payments Protocol) — they are distinct.

q: Should I read account balances and history from Stellar RPC or from Hubble/BigQuery? When does each make sense?

must_have.claim: Use RPC for live/current-state and hot reads (it is rate-limited and not built for large historical scans).
Use Hubble (BigQuery) for bulk historical SQL analytics across the full network record.

should_have.claim: An indexer covers the in-between window (filtered historical queries beyond RPC's limited event window — 24h default, ~7 days max).
Choosing the wrong layer inflates cost — RPC for historical scans is rate-limited; BigQuery for low-latency reads is wasteful.

nice_to_have.claim: Per-account current state can come from RPC `getLedgerEntries` or Horizon /accounts/{address}.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT recommend RPC for bulk historical scans or Hubble/BigQuery for low-latency single-account live reads.

must_cite: developers.stellar.org RPC, Hubble, and/or indexers docs.
notes: Three-layer (RPC / indexer / Hubble) decision; the trap is using the wrong layer for the workload. RPC event window is 24h default / ~7 days max (not a flat 7d).

q: How does Soroban verify passkey (WebAuthn) signatures on-chain — what's the role of secp256r1?

must_have.claim: Soroban exposes a secp256r1 (P-256) signature-verification host function so contracts can verify WebAuthn/passkey signatures inside their auth check.
secp256r1 is the curve used by WebAuthn/passkeys, which is why on-chain verification is needed for passkey smart wallets.

should_have.claim: A smart-wallet contract account checks the signature in `__check_auth`/`check_auth`; signers can be secp256r1 (passkey), Ed25519, or policy signers.

nice_to_have.claim: This signer model replaces trust in a single root key with multiple/custom signer types.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT confuse secp256r1 (P-256, used by passkeys) with secp256k1 (Bitcoin/Ethereum's curve) or Ed25519 (classic Stellar keys).

must_cite: developers.stellar.org smart-wallets / contract-accounts guide.
notes: Verified against the smart-wallets guide: registration stores a WebAuthn (secp256r1/P-256/ES256) public key in contract state; in __check_auth the contract verifies the secp256r1 signature and applies policy checks. secp256r1 vs secp256k1 vs Ed25519 mix-up is the defining trap.

q: What is the base reserve on Stellar and how does it determine an account's minimum XLM balance?

should_have.claim: Notes subentries that consume reserves include trustlines, offers, signers, and data entries (each adds one base reserve).

q: Walk me through Stellar's protocol upgrade history from Protocol 19 through the latest version, with the headline feature of each.

must_have.claim: Protocol 19 (2022) introduced generalized transaction preconditions / timelocks (CAP-0021).
Protocol 20 introduced Soroban smart contracts (CAP-0046 series), live on Mainnet 2024-02-20.
Protocol 21 added secp256r1/passkey verification (CAP-0051) and TTL extension.
Protocol 22 added BLS12-381 host functions (CAP-0059) and contract constructors (CAP-0058).
Protocol 23 ('Whisk') added parallel smart-contract execution (CAP-0063) and live/archival state separation (CAP-0062).
Identifies the latest activated version as Protocol 26 ('Yardstick', Soroban cost-model tuning) as of the 2026-06-22 snapshot, with Protocol 27 (CAP-0071) queued.

should_have.claim: Notes Protocol 24 was a stability/corrective upgrade fixing Whisk's state-archival bug.
Notes Protocol 25 ('X-Ray') added BN254 and Poseidon/Poseidon2 host functions (ZK groundwork).

Do NOT invent protocol versions or CAP numbers not in the history.
Do NOT present the latest version as fixed truth without a freshness caveat.

q: What are the three SCF Build tracks (Open, Integration, RFP) and how do they differ?

must_have.claim: SCF Build (v7.0) has three tracks: Open, Integration, and RFP.
Open Track is the only track with a community vote (NQG); Integration and RFP are panel-only.

should_have.claim: Open Track is for brand-new builds; Integration Track is for integrating existing Stellar building blocks / projects with traction.
RFP Track is for tooling/SDKs/APIs/explorers aligned with published SCF RFPs.

nice_to_have.claim: All three tracks share the $150K XLM cap; there is also a Resubmission path.

Do NOT invent additional Build tracks beyond Open/Integration/RFP (+Resubmission).

q: How is an SCF Build Award disbursed across milestones under the current (v7.0) model?

must_have.claim: Under SCF v7.0 the Build Award is disbursed in four tranches tied to milestones.

should_have.claim: This 4-tranche model replaced the prior v6.0 three-equal-disbursement structure.

nice_to_have.claim: Funding is intended to cover roughly four months of development.

q: How does SCF community voting work — what is Neural Quorum Governance and who can vote?

must_have.claim: SCF community voting uses Neural Quorum Governance (NQG), a reputation-weighted voting mechanism.
Only SCF Verified Members can vote in the community vote.
NQG combines neural governance (reputation-weighted voting power) with quorum delegation (delegating to trusted quorums).

should_have.claim: NQG was adopted by SCF in October 2023 and was co-developed with BlockScience.
The community vote applies to the Build Open Track (and the Public Goods Award).

nice_to_have.claim: Delegate selection cutoff is roughly 1/40 of total NQG voting weight for a track.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT describe SCF voting as one-token-one-vote / XLM-balance-weighted or open to anyone holding XLM.
Do NOT claim NQG is a token or coin, or that it is the same as Stellar's protocol validator quorum sets (SCP).

must_cite: The SCF handbook Neural Quorum Governance / Verified Members pages.
notes: Dossier §3. Key trap: NQG (SCF grant governance) vs SCP quorum sets (protocol consensus) — different things despite both saying 'quorum'.

q: What is SEP-1 and where is the stellar.toml file served?

must_have.claim: SEP-1 defines the stellar.toml (Stellar Info File) used to publish an org's Stellar metadata and endpoints.
It is served at https://<domain>/.well-known/stellar.toml.

should_have.claim: It is the discovery layer wallets use to find an anchor's SEP endpoints, currencies, and signing keys.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes it can declare validators, principals, and an approval_server (for SEP-8).

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT misnumber the info-file standard (it is SEP-1, not SEP-2/SEP-10).
Do NOT claim stellar.toml lives on-chain rather than at a web .well-known path.

must_cite: SEP-0001 on the stellar-protocol repo or developers.stellar.org.

q: How does SEP-10 authenticate a Stellar wallet to an anchor?

must_have.claim: Names SEP-10 as Stellar Web Authentication.
The wallet signs a server-provided challenge transaction to prove control of its account; the server returns a JWT on success.

should_have.claim: The challenge transaction is not submitted to the network (it is a signed proof, not a real payment).

nice_to_have.claim: Notes SEP-45 extends web auth to Soroban contract accounts.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT misnumber it (authentication is SEP-10, not SEP-12/SEP-24).
Do NOT claim the challenge transaction is submitted on-chain to authenticate.

must_cite: SEP-0010 on the stellar-protocol repo or developers.stellar.org.
notes: Dossier §7.1, Q12. Verified: SEP-0010 'Stellar Authentication', Status Active (ecosystem README master table).

q: Which SEP defines how a wallet submits KYC data to an anchor, and which SEP provides the standard field names?

must_have.claim: Names SEP-12 as the KYC API (POST /customer, status callbacks) for sending customer PII/AML data to an anchor.
Names SEP-9 as the Standard KYC Fields data dictionary that SEP-12 uses.

should_have.claim: The anchor verifies the data and returns a status; SEP-12 is used by SEP-6/24/31 flows.

q: What does SEP-31 do and how is it different from SEP-24?

must_have.claim: Names SEP-31 as the Cross-Border Payments API — a sending anchor to a receiving anchor (anchor-to-anchor) flow.
Contrasts SEP-24 as wallet↔anchor interactive (hosted) deposit/withdrawal for an end user, not anchor-to-anchor.

should_have.claim: In SEP-31 the sending anchor collects sender KYC and supplies receiver info; there is no end-user hosted webview.
Both can use SEP-38 for quotes and SEP-10 for auth.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes SEP-31 underpins remittance/disbursement corridors (e.g. via SDP).

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT call SEP-31 the wallet/anchor interactive deposit-withdraw standard (that is SEP-24).

q: How does SEP-8 add per-transaction approval for regulated assets, and what does it require in stellar.toml?

must_have.claim: Names SEP-8 as the Regulated Assets standard, adding per-transaction issuer approval.
Requires an approval_server declared in the issuer's stellar.toml, to which each transaction is POSTed for approval.

should_have.claim: The issuer must set both AUTH_REQUIRED and AUTH_REVOCABLE flags so it can authorize/deauthorize per transaction.
Approval-server responses include statuses success / revised / pending / action_required / rejected.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes SEP-57 (T-REX) is an emerging next-gen regulated-asset standard.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT misnumber it (regulated assets is SEP-8, not SEP-12 or SEP-31).
Do NOT claim SEP-8 is account-level flags only with no approval server.

q: I set AUTH_CLAWBACK_ENABLED on my issuing account but clawback isn't sticking — what flag am I missing?

must_have.claim: AUTH_CLAWBACK_ENABLED only takes effect if AUTH_REVOCABLE is also set on the account.

should_have.claim: Clawback applies to trustlines opened after the flag was set; existing trustlines may not become clawback-able retroactively.
Per-trustline clawback state can be managed via SetTrustLineFlags.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes XLM/native balances are never clawback-able.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT claim AUTH_CLAWBACK_ENABLED works on its own without AUTH_REVOCABLE.
Do NOT invent a missing flag name that does not exist.

must_cite: The clawback page on developers.stellar.org or CAP-0035.
notes: Applied troubleshooting how-to. Dossier §1.3, §1.4, Q4. Flag-ordering gotcha verified against docs + CAP-0035.
criteria: Raven SHOULD answer. Expected cards: stellar_docs_mcp (or any acceptable alternative — route-pass-equivalent: scout_research). must_have.claim: Names SEP-24 as the interactive (anchor-hosted UI / webview) deposit & withdrawal standard.
Contrasts SEP-6 as the programmatic (API-driven, no hosted UI) deposit/withdrawal counterpart.

Do NOT claim SEP-31 is the wallet/anchor interactive standard (SEP-31 is cross-border anchor-to-anchor).

q: Does Soroban support delegated signers for custom accounts, and which protocol version adds it?

must_have.claim: Auth delegation for custom accounts is introduced in Protocol 27 ('Stellar Zipper') via CAP-0071, adding the `SOROBAN_CREDENTIALS_ADDRESS_WITH_DELEGATES` credential type (nested `SorobanDelegateSignature` delegation) for custom accounts.
Protocol 27 also adds `SOROBAN_CREDENTIALS_ADDRESS_V2`, an address-bound credential that prevents cross-account signature replay when keys are shared.

should_have.claim: Answer should flag the version/status (testnet ~June 2026, mainnet vote scheduled ~July 8 2026) rather than assert it is already live everywhere.
Requires the matching `soroban-sdk`/`stellar-cli` versions that support CAP-71 (SDK v26.x, CLI v27.x).

nice_to_have.claim: Notes the original `SOROBAN_CREDENTIALS_ADDRESS` is slated for deprecation in a later protocol (P28).

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT assert delegated signers were always available / available since Soroban launch.
Do NOT attribute delegation to the wrong protocol (e.g., P22, P25) or claim mainnet activation as already final without the vote.

must_cite: The Protocol 27 / Stellar Zipper upgrade guide or CAP-0071 reference.

The CAP text does NOT name a `delegate_account_auth` host fn (that label is from secondary sources), so the rubric only gates on the credential types. P27 timing (testnet/mainnet vote) is freshness-sensitive.

q: In Soroban, what's the difference between authorization and authentication, and which does require_auth handle?

must_have.claim: Authentication = confirming identity (who you are); authorization = judging whether an operation is permitted.
`require_auth` asserts that a given `Address` has authorized the current action; the host handles verifying the authentication (signatures) behind that.

should_have.claim: For classic accounts the host checks signatures against the account; for contract (custom) accounts it delegates the check to the contract's `__check_auth`.

nice_to_have.claim: Contract-level business rules (roles/permissions) are still the contract's responsibility on top of `require_auth`.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT claim `require_auth` performs no signature verification (the host does verify).
Do NOT conflate the two terms as identical or swap their definitions.

must_cite: The developers.stellar.org authorization fundamentals page.
notes: Conceptual comparison straight from the auth docs; definitions and the __check_auth delegation verified verbatim against the authorization fundamentals page.

Should include: For classic accounts the host checks signatures against the account; for contract (custom) accounts it delegates the check to the contract's `__check_auth`.. q: How can I verify a deployed Soroban contract's WASM matches its published source, and which Stellar standards cover it?

must_have.claim: Verification works by building the WASM in CI and producing GitHub Attestations (SEP-55, e.g. the soroban-build-workflow) and/or by reproducibly rebuilding it and comparing bytes (SEP-58).
The WASM carries contractmetav0 metadata (e.g. a source_repo / source_sha256 entry) linking it to the source.
Build/verification info is surfaced in tooling such as Stellar Lab / explorers (Build Info) or `stellar contract info meta`.
The relevant standards are SEP-55 (Contract Build Verification, Draft) and SEP-58 (Contract Build Reproducibility for Verification, Draft).

should_have.claim: 'Build verified' attests that the published build workflow produced the WASM, not that the source is safe/audited.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes these SEPs are Draft and subject to change.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT call contract build verification SEP-49 (SEP-49 is Upgradeable Contracts).
Do NOT claim Stellar stores the contract source code on-chain.

VERIFIED against the SEP repo (2026-06): SEP-55 = 'Contract Build Verification' (Draft, v0.4.1), attestation-based via GitHub Attestations + contractmetav0 source_repo; SEP-58 = 'Contract Build Reproducibility for Verification' (Draft, v0.5.0), rebuild-based via bldimg/bldopt/source_sha256 meta, explicitly complementary to SEP-55. q: How is a Soroban contract's address (the C... id) determined at deploy time, and is it deterministic?

must_have.claim: The contract address (a `C...` strkey) is a deterministic hash derived from the deployer/source plus a salt (the ContractIDPreimage).
The same deployer + same salt yields the same contract address; varying the salt yields distinct addresses.

should_have.claim: The address is NOT derived from the Wasm hash itself, so upgrading the Wasm does not change the address.
Upload (Wasm hash) and create/deploy (instance address) are separate steps.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes this enables counterfactual / pre-computed addresses for factory patterns.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT claim the address is the Ethereum-style `keccak(sender, nonce)` / CREATE2 of the bytecode.
Do NOT claim the contract id changes when you upgrade the Wasm.

must_cite: A developers.stellar.org page on contract creation / addresses / deployment.
notes: Address = SHA-256 over ContractIDPreimage (CONTRACT_ID_PREIMAGE_FROM_ADDRESS: deployer address + salt) plus network id; NOT the Wasm hash. Upload vs create are separate ops. Verified against the Stellar transaction fundamentals page.

`#[contractevent]` defines a typed contract event whose topics/data are published at runtime via `env.events()`.

q: How does one Soroban contract call a function on another deployed contract?

must_have.claim: A contract invokes another by its contract address, either via a generated client (from `contractimport!`/`stellar contract bindings`) or via `env.invoke_contract(...)`.
The generated typed-client approach is preferred so the callee's spec is type-checked at build time.

q: My cross-contract call fails with a footprint/HostError at runtime even though it simulates the logic correctly. Why?

must_have.claim: Every storage entry read/written across the whole call chain must be declared in the transaction footprint; a missing entry causes a runtime HostError.
The footprint is normally computed by simulating the transaction (RPC `simulateTransaction` / `stellar tx simulate`), which discovers the entries the inner calls touch.

should_have.claim: Soroban uses deterministic, explicitly-declared footprints (read-only + read-write sets) so validators can parallelize.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes that auth entries for inner-contract `require_auth` must also be provided/authorized.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT claim Soroban auto-discovers all touched state at execution time with no footprint needed.
Do NOT attribute this to gas-limit/out-of-gas when it is a footprint declaration issue.

notes: Multi-hop: cross-contract + footprint + simulation. Trap: thinking footprints are auto-discovered at exec. Verified: Soroban requires an explicitly-declared read/write footprint covering the whole call chain; simulation computes it.
criteria: Raven SHOULD answer. Expected cards: stellar_docs_mcp (or any acceptable alternative — route-pass-equivalent: scout_research, scout_repos). q: How are Soroban transaction fees structured, and which part can be refunded?

must_have.claim: Soroban fees split into an inclusion (bid) fee plus a resource fee; the resource fee has a non-refundable portion and a refundable portion.
The refundable portion is returned based on actual on-chain usage after execution.

should_have.claim: The non-refundable portion covers CPU instructions, read/write bytes, and bandwidth/size; the refundable portion covers rent, events, and return values (charged up front, refunded by actual usage).
Fees are denominated in stroops (1 XLM = 10,000,000 stroops).

q: Why is storing a growing per-user map in Instance storage a denial-of-service risk in Soroban?

must_have.claim: Instance storage is deserialized/loaded on EVERY contract invocation, so an unbounded-growing Instance map makes every call more expensive until it hits the ledger-entry size limit and bricks the contract.
The fix is to put per-user/unbounded data in Persistent storage keyed per-user, reserving Instance for small fixed config.

should_have.claim: This is a recognized Soroban vulnerability class (Instance-storage DoS / unbounded storage growth) in audit checklists.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes a single Persistent key accumulating unbounded array entries has a similar blowup risk and should be sharded.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT claim Instance storage is loaded lazily/only-when-accessed like Persistent (it is loaded every call).
Do NOT frame this primarily as a reentrancy bug (it is a storage-growth/DoS class).

must_cite: A Soroban security checklist (e.g., Veridise) or developers.stellar.org storage guidance.
notes: Routes to scout_research (security corpus). Instance = whole instance entry loaded per call; verified against the storage/state-archival semantics. Trap: thinking Instance loads lazily.

q: How does OpenZeppelin's Upgradeable module for Stellar make a Soroban contract upgradeable?

must_have.claim: OpenZeppelin's Upgradeable module provides derive macros (e.g., `Upgradeable` / `UpgradeableMigratable`) that wire up an admin-gated `upgrade` entrypoint over native Wasm replacement (`update_current_contract_wasm`).
It uses Soroban's in-place Wasm-replacement model (same address/state preserved), NOT an EVM-style proxy/delegatecall.

should_have.claim: Upgrades must be access-controlled (only an authorized admin/role can call upgrade).
Storage-key layout must remain compatible across the upgrade; a migrate hook handles state changes atomically.

nice_to_have.claim: References an Upgrader contract pattern for atomic upgrade-and-migrate.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT describe a transparent/UUPS proxy with delegatecall as the OZ-for-Stellar mechanism.
Do NOT claim the upgrade changes the contract address or wipes storage.

q: How do I emit an event from a Soroban contract and how long can I read it back afterward?

must_have.claim: Emit events with `env.events().publish((topics...), data)` (optionally via the typed `#[contractevent]` macro).
Events are written into the transaction meta and read off-chain via Stellar RPC `getEvents`.

should_have.claim: RPC retains queryable events for a limited window (about 7 days); for older events you need an external indexer.
The leading topics are indexed and used to filter event subscriptions.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes events are dropped if the transaction reverts.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT claim events are stored on-ledger forever and freely queryable for all history from RPC.
Do NOT describe Solidity `emit`/`event` syntax or `indexed` keyword as the Soroban mechanism.

must_cite: The developers.stellar.org events / RPC getEvents documentation.
notes: Retention (~7 days) is the key fact; 'forever queryable' is the trap. Verified: events published via env.events().publish, read via RPC getEvents with a bounded retention window (~7 days).

q: Is reentrancy a concern in Soroban smart contracts the way it is on Ethereum?

must_have.claim: Classic single-contract reentrancy is largely prevented by the Soroban host: a contract cannot be re-entered while it is mid-execution (the host disallows reentry into an executing contract).
Therefore the dominant Soroban risks are different (storage-growth DoS, auth/footprint mistakes, oracle/replay), not Ethereum-style reentrancy.

should_have.claim: You should still reason about cross-contract call ordering and state assumptions (checks-effects-interactions-style discipline) for composed flows.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes you generally do not need a Solidity-style ReentrancyGuard/nonReentrant modifier on Soroban.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT claim Soroban needs a Solidity-style `nonReentrant`/ReentrancyGuard as the primary defense because reentrancy works the same as on Ethereum.
Do NOT claim Soroban has zero call-ordering / composition risks whatsoever.

must_cite: A Soroban security source (Veridise/Inferara/SDF) on the reentrancy/host model.
notes: Soroban host disallows re-entering a contract already on the call stack, so classic single-contract reentrancy is structurally prevented; dominant risks are storage-growth DoS / auth / oracle. Trap: asserting EVM-identical reentrancy and prescribing nonReentrant.
criteria: Raven SHOULD answer. Expected cards: scout_research (or any acceptable alternative — route-pass-equivalent: stellar_docs_mcp, scout_repos). MUST AVOID (gate): Do NOT claim Soroban needs a Solidity-style `nonReentrant`/ReentrancyGuard as the primary defense because reentrancy works the same as on Ethereum.; Do NOT claim Soroban has zero call-ordering / composition risks whatsoever.. q: How do I require that a caller authorized an action in a Soroban contract?

must_have.claim: Call `address.require_auth()` (or `require_auth_for_args(args)`) on the `Address` whose authorization is needed.
The host verifies the authorization payload/signatures against the transaction's SorobanCredentials at that call site.

should_have.claim: `require_auth_for_args` lets you bind the authorization to a specific subset of arguments.
Replay is prevented via a host-managed nonce + ledger expiration in the auth payload.

nice_to_have.claim: In tests, `env.mock_all_auths()` bypasses real signatures.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT recommend `msg.sender`, `tx.origin`, or `onlyOwner` modifiers (those are Solidity, not Soroban).
Do NOT claim Soroban authorization is just checking the transaction source account / no per-address auth exists.

must_cite: The developers.stellar.org authorization documentation.
notes: msg.sender/tx.origin is the canonical EVM trap. Verified: address.require_auth()/require_auth_for_args; host verifies SorobanCredentials; replay via host-managed nonce + ledger expiration.

q: What resource limits can cause a Soroban transaction to fail, like CPU instructions, memory, and ledger entries?

must_have.claim: There are per-transaction (and per-ledger) caps on CPU instructions, Wasm memory, read/write ledger entries, and read/write bytes; exceeding them fails the transaction.
These limits are network configuration settings that can change via protocol upgrades, so exact numbers should be flagged as version-dependent.

should_have.claim: The Wasm memory limit is a hard ceiling with no fee (you cannot pay to exceed it).
Hitting a resource limit produces a failed transaction at execution, not a soft warning.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes ledger limits (e.g., ledgerMaxInstructions) gate how many contract txs fit in a ledger.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT cite a single fixed 'block gas limit' as in Ethereum as the model.
Do NOT present specific limit numbers as permanently fixed constants without noting they are config-tunable.

must_cite: The developers.stellar.org fees/resource-limits/metering page (config settings).

q: My contract's Persistent storage entry got archived. How do I restore it so I can use the contract again?

must_have.claim: Archived Persistent/Instance entries are restored using a restore operation (`RestoreFootprintOp`) and/or the CLI `stellar contract restore`.
The entry to restore must be named in the transaction's restore footprint.

should_have.claim: Since CAP-0066 (Protocol 23), `InvokeHostFunction` can auto-restore archived entries listed in its restore footprint within the same transaction.
After restoring, you should extend the entry's TTL (`extend_ttl()`) to keep it live.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes restoring costs a fee proportional to the entry size.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT say archived Persistent data is gone forever / unrecoverable.
Do NOT advise re-deploying the whole contract as the only way to recover archived state.
Do NOT confuse this with Temporary entries (which are not restorable).

must_cite: The developers.stellar.org state-archival / restore guide (and CLI restore reference).
notes: Freshness: auto-restore is CAP-0066/P23-gated. VERIFIED against state-archival docs (2026-06): archived Persistent/Instance entries are restorable; since Protocol 23 InvokeHostFunction auto-restores entries in the restore footprint (populated by RPC simulation), so RestoreFootprintOp is 'for the most part no longer needed'; Temporary is NOT restorable.

q: Where do SAC token balances live for a classic G-account versus a contract holder, and what integer types are used?

must_have.claim: For a classic `Address::Account` (G-account) holder, the SAC balance lives in the account's trustline as an i64.
For an `Address::Contract` (C-address) holder, the SAC balance lives in contract storage as an i128.

should_have.claim: This means a G-account must have a trustline (or use the native asset) to hold the SAC balance, while contract holders do not use trustlines.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes Protocol 26 / CAP-73 adds a SAC `trust` function so a contract can open a trustline for a G-account.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT claim all SAC balances are stored uniformly in contract storage regardless of holder type.
Do NOT swap the integer widths (e.g., say trustlines are i128 and contract balances i64).

q: Should I launch a token as a Stellar Asset Contract or write a custom SEP-41 contract token? What are the tradeoffs?

must_have.claim: Both expose the SEP-41 interface; a SAC is the built-in, audited bridge for a classic asset, while a custom contract token is hand-written Rust for programmable logic.

Choose SAC for a standard asset (simplicity, classic-asset interop, no custom code/audit); choose a custom SEP-41 token when you need extra logic (fee-on-transfer, hooks, vesting, complex compliance).

should_have.claim: A SAC inherits classic-asset features (issuer auth flags, clawback, trustlines); a custom token re-implements whatever it needs.
Custom tokens carry their own audit burden; OpenZeppelin's SEP-41 base reduces it.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes regulated/RWA cases may prefer an ERC-3643-style contract token over a plain SAC.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT claim SAC and custom tokens are incompatible / use different non-interoperable interfaces (both speak SEP-41).
Do NOT recommend always writing a custom token (SAC is the default for standard assets).

notes: Comparison; trap is claiming incompatibility or always-custom. Verified: SAC is the built-in CAP-46-6/SEP-41 wrapper for classic assets; custom tokens also implement SEP-41; choose by whether you need programmable logic.

q: What do the #[contract], #[contractimpl], and #[contracttype] macros do in the Soroban SDK?

must_have.claim: `#[contract]` marks the contract struct (type), and `#[contractimpl]` marks the impl block whose `pub` functions become the callable contract entrypoints / spec.
`#[contracttype]` lets a custom struct/enum cross the host boundary by generating its SCVal/XDR encoding.

should_have.claim: Contract functions take `&Env` (or `Env`) as their first argument.
`#[contractimpl]` generates the contract spec used to produce client bindings.

q: What are the three Soroban storage types and when should I use each?

must_have.claim: The three storage types are Instance, Persistent, and Temporary (accessed via `env.storage()`).
Persistent is for long-lived data like balances/ownership; Temporary is for cheap short-lived data; Instance is for per-contract config/admin data tied to the contract instance.

should_have.claim: Instance storage is loaded on every contract invocation (so keep it small).
Temporary is the cheapest tier.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes Instance data shares its TTL with the contract instance entry.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT invent storage types not in Soroban (e.g., only 'storage'/'memory'/'calldata' as in Solidity).
Do NOT say Temporary data is restorable after expiry, or that Persistent data is permanently deleted on expiry (those are swapped).

notes: Storage-semantics swap is the headline trap; Solidity storage/memory/calldata is the cross-chain trap. Verified against 'choosing the right storage' + state-archival docs.

q: What's the correct pattern for moving SAC/SEP-41 tokens from inside a Soroban contract, and what authorization does it need?

must_have.claim: Use a typed token client (the SAC/SEP-41 interface) and call `transfer(from, to, amount)`; the `from` address's `require_auth` must be satisfied for the transfer to authorize.

When the contract itself holds and sends tokens, the contract's own address is the `from`, and the contract authorizes by invoking the transfer (no external user signature needed for the contract's own balance).

should_have.claim: Amounts are i128; validate non-negative/within-range amounts to avoid overflow/abort.
Follow checks-effects-interactions discipline: update internal state before/around external token calls.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT use an ERC-20 `approve`+`transferFrom` allowance dance as the required pattern (Soroban authorizes via `require_auth`, though SEP-41 does have allowance methods).

q: What happens to Persistent vs Temporary storage entries when their TTL runs out?

must_have.claim: When a Persistent (or Instance) entry's TTL expires it is archived, NOT deleted, and can be restored later.
When a Temporary entry's TTL expires it is permanently deleted and cannot be restored.

should_have.claim: TTL is the number of ledgers until `liveUntilLedger`; you extend it with `extend_ttl()`.
Archived Persistent/Instance entries are restored via a restore footprint / RestoreFootprintOp (auto-restore available since CAP-0066 / Protocol 23).

nice_to_have.claim: Notes you should proactively extend TTL on critical entries to avoid eviction.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT claim Temporary data can be restored after expiry.
Do NOT claim Persistent data is permanently deleted (lost) when TTL hits zero.
Do NOT describe TTL as a wall-clock timer rather than a ledger count.

must_cite: The developers.stellar.org state-archival documentation.
notes: The defining TTL-behavior trap: restorable vs permanently-deleted by tier. VERIFIED against state-archival docs: Temporary is permanently deleted/non-restorable on expiry; Persistent & Instance are archived & restorable; auto-restore since P23/CAP-0066. TTL is in ledgers (liveUntilLedger).
criteria: Raven SHOULD answer. Expected cards: stellar_docs_mcp (or any acceptable alternative — route-pass-equivalent: scout_research). q: How do I upgrade a deployed Soroban contract's code without changing its contract address?

must_have.claim: Soroban supports in-place Wasm replacement: upload the new Wasm, then have the contract call `env.deployer().update_current_contract_wasm(new_wasm_hash)` to swap its code while keeping the same address and storage.
The upgrade entrypoint must be gated by authorization (e.g., an admin `require_auth`) so only an authorized party can upgrade.

should_have.claim: Storage/state persists across the upgrade, so the new code must keep storage-key layout compatible.
The new Wasm must first be uploaded (`stellar contract upload`) to get its hash.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes a proxy/data-vs-impl split is an alternative pattern, but native Wasm replacement is the canonical Soroban way (no separate proxy needed).

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT claim you must redeploy to a new address (losing state) to change a contract's code.
Do NOT prescribe an EVM proxy pattern (delegatecall/UUPS/transparent proxy) as the required Soroban mechanism.
Do NOT omit that the upgrade must be access-controlled.

q: If a user loses a passkey for a Stellar smart wallet, what recovery patterns exist (backup signers, policy signers, multi-signer design)?

must_have.claim: Stellar smart wallets / custom accounts (Soroban) support multiple signers and policy signers.
Recovery comes from designing in backup/secondary signers and multisig thresholds, so a lost passkey is one of several keys, not the only one.

should_have.claim: Session keys / a relayer can also be part of the recovery/operation design.
passkey-kit / smart-account-kit support these multi-signer / policy-signer patterns.

nice_to_have.claim: Notes the recovery flow is: authenticate with a remaining signer, add a new passkey, remove the lost one.

must_avoid.claim: Do NOT claim a lost passkey means irrecoverable loss of the wallet/funds.
Do NOT suggest seed-phrase / BIP-39 recovery — passkeys are not BIP-39 seeds.

must_cite: A primary developers.stellar.org smart-wallet / custom-account source (and/or passkey-kit docs).
notes: Verified: the smart-wallets guide stores a WebAuthn (secp256r1) public key in contract state and verifies it in __check_auth with policy checks; signers can be secp256r1/Ed25519/policy. smart-account-kit exposes addPasskey/addDelegated/remove — the design supports adding a new passkey and removing a lost one (recovery via backup/secondary signers), confirming a lost passkey is NOT irrecoverable. Canonical URL fixed (old /docs/build/smart-wallets redirects).
criteria: Raven SHOULD answer. Expected cards: stellar_docs_mcp (or any acceptable alternative — route-pass-equivalent: scout_repos). criteria: Raven SHOULD answer. Expected cards: stellar_docs_mcp (or any acceptable alternative — route-pass-equivalent: scout_repos, scout_research). MUST include (gate): SDF-maintained client/contract SDKs are JS (`@stellar/stellar-sdk`), Go (`go-stellar-sdk`), and the Rust crates (`soroban-sdk`, `stellar-xdr`, `stellar-strkey`, `rs-stellar-rpc-client`).; Community-maintained SDKs include Python (StellarCN), Java (lightsail-network), Flutter/iOS/Kotlin/PHP (Soneso), and .NET (Beans-BV).; Distinguishes contract SDKs (build Soroban Wasm) from client/XDR SDKs (talk to Horizon/RPC, sign, decode XDR).. MUST AVOID (gate): Do NOT label community SDKs (Python/Java/Flutter/.NET) as SDF-maintained or vice-versa.; Do NOT claim there is only one SDK or that all SDKs are SDF-maintained.. Should include: Recommends defaulting to SDF-maintained SDKs for production/institutional workloads, treating community SDKs as best-effort.. Must cite: developers.stellar.org client-sdks and contract-sdks pages..
